content.description: A truly global, decentralized graph where data objects can be scattered anywhere and reassembled by any agent that discovers them. Extract docs: cat THIS_FILE | jq -r '.item.instruction' > dataverse.md
content.name: dataverse001

item.instruction:
# Dataverse001 Protocol Spec

A truly global, decentralized graph where data objects can be scattered anywhere - files, disk slack space, steganography in images - and reassembled by any agent that discovers them.

## Core Concept

Every object is a self-contained JSON fragment with `"in": "dataverse001"` as a magic marker. When any device encounters this marker, it knows the object belongs to the global graph and can fold it into its local view.

### Transport Agnostic

Objects can travel via any channel: APIs, DHTs, Bluetooth mesh, LoRa, QR codes, steganography, blockchains. It doesn't matter how you obtain the data - you can use it all to discover parts of the graph. If internet gets cut off, the graph can still propagate through local mesh networks.

## Object Format

```json
{
  "in": "dataverse001",
  "signature": "<base64>",
  "item": {
    "id": "<uuid>",
    "pubkey": "<compressed-raw-pubkey-base64url>",
    "created_at": "<iso8601>",
    "updated_at": "<iso8601>",
    "revision": 0,
    "type": "POST",
    "instruction": "A post. Display title and body.",
    "relations": {
      "posted_in": [{"ref": "<pubkey>.<uuid>"}],
      "author": [{"ref": "<pubkey>.<uuid>"}]
    },
    "content": {
      "title": "Hello Dataverse",
      "body": "First post!"
    }
  }
}
```

## Object Identity

Objects are uniquely identified by the composite key `(pubkey, id)`. This prevents UUID squatting - you can only create objects under your own pubkey namespace.

### Composite Key Format

The composite key is expressed as a single string: `{pubkey}.{id}`

Example: `AxyU5_5vWmP2tO_klN4UpbZzRsuJEvJTrdwdg_gODxZJ.346bef5e-94ff-4f7a-bcf6-d78ae1e1541c`

This format:
- Uses `.` as delimiter (URL-safe, filesystem-safe, not in base64url or UUIDs)
- Is the same as the filename (minus `.json` extension)
- Enables direct file lookup from relation values

### Relations

Relations are arrays of objects with a `ref` field containing the composite key:

```json
"relations": {
  "author": [{"ref": "AxyU5_5vWmP2tO_klN4UpbZzRsuJEvJTrdwdg_gODxZJ.346bef5e-94ff-4f7a-bcf6-d78ae1e1541c"}],
  "replies_to": [{"ref": "BzxY7_2wQnR3sK_mAbCdEfGhIjKlMnOpQrStUvWxYz.a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890", "title": "Parent post"}]
}
```

- `ref` (required): The composite key `{pubkey}.{id}`
- `revision` (optional): Revision of the target object. Defaults to latest object. Using the latest object is desirable as not all nodes retain older versions, and older versions usually don't spread. Only use if necessary.
- Additional fields (optional): `title`, `summary`, or any other hints

To parse a composite key: `split('.')` → `[pubkey, id]`

### Required Fields

- **in**: Magic marker `"dataverse001"` - identifies this as a dataverse object
- **signature**: Signs the `item` object (envelope pattern)
- **item.id**: UUID
- **item.pubkey**: Creator's public key (compressed raw, base64url)
- **item.created_at**: ISO8601 timestamp (`Z` suffix preferred, offsets like `+01:00` accepted)

### Optional Fields

- **item.updated_at**: Timestamp of last modification
- **item.revision**: Integer counter, incremented on update (higher wins on sync)
- **item.type**: e.g. `POST`, `COMMENT`, `IDENTITY`, `ROOT`, `TYPE`
- **item.instruction**: Tells agents how to interpret/display this object
- **item.relations**: Named arrays of composite key strings
- **item.content**: Free-form payload (title, body, text, etc.)

## Cryptography

- ECC P-256 (prime256v1) with ECDSA for signing
- All items are signed by their creator
- `pubkey` field = compressed raw public key (33 bytes, base64url encoded)

### Public Key Format

The `pubkey` field contains the **compressed raw EC point** encoded as base64url (no padding):

- **Format**: `02|03 || X` (33 bytes) - EC point in compressed form
- **Encoding**: base64url (RFC 4648 §5) - uses `-` and `_`, no `=` padding
- **Result**: 44 characters

### Generating a Keypair

```bash
# Generate private key
openssl ecparam -genkey -name prime256v1 -noout -out private.pem

# Extract compressed raw pubkey as base64url
openssl ec -in private.pem -pubout -conv_form compressed -outform DER 2>/dev/null \
  | tail -c 33 | base64 | tr '+/' '-_' | tr -d '='
```

### Signing

**IMPORTANT**: Sign using **canonical JSON**: compact, sorted keys (`jq -cS`), no trailing newline.

```bash
echo "$ITEM" | jq -cS '.' | tr -d '\n' > /tmp/item.json
openssl dgst -sha256 -sign private.pem /tmp/item.json | base64 -w0
```

### Verifying

P-256 compressed keys need a fixed 26-byte DER header for OpenSSL:

```bash
DER_HEADER="3039301306072a8648ce3d020106082a8648ce3d030107032200"
```

### Verify Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# verify - Verify signature of a dataverse001 object
# Usage: ./verify <file.json>

FILE="${1:?Usage: ./verify <file.json>}"
DER_HEADER="3039301306072a8648ce3d020106082a8648ce3d030107032200"

# Extract and canonicalize item
jq -cS '.item' "$FILE" | tr -d '\n' > /tmp/dv_item.json

# Extract signature
jq -r '.signature' "$FILE" | base64 -d > /tmp/dv_sig.bin

# Extract pubkey, convert base64url to base64
PUBKEY_B64URL=$(jq -r '.item.pubkey' "$FILE")
PUBKEY_B64=$(echo "$PUBKEY_B64URL" | tr '_-' '/+')
case $((${#PUBKEY_B64} % 4)) in
  2) PUBKEY_B64="${PUBKEY_B64}==" ;;
  3) PUBKEY_B64="${PUBKEY_B64}=" ;;
esac
PUBKEY_HEX=$(echo "$PUBKEY_B64" | base64 -d | xxd -p -c 100)

# Reconstruct DER and convert to PEM
echo -n "${DER_HEADER}${PUBKEY_HEX}" | xxd -r -p > /tmp/dv_pub.der
openssl ec -pubin -inform DER -in /tmp/dv_pub.der -outform PEM -out /tmp/dv_pub.pem 2>/dev/null

# Verify
openssl dgst -sha256 -verify /tmp/dv_pub.pem -signature /tmp/dv_sig.bin /tmp/dv_item.json
```

## Design Principles

- Objects are small, self-contained, discoverable
- Graph structure with named relations
- Composite key `(pubkey, id)` prevents namespace squatting
- One object per composite key, overwrites allowed (latest revision wins on sync)

### Self-Describing Objects

Objects should contain enough context for any LLM or human to interpret them. The `instruction` field explains how to use/display the object.

For repeated types, use a `type_def` relation to reference the TYPE definition:

```json
"relations": { "type_def": [{"ref": "<pubkey>.<uuid>"}] }
```

The `type_def` points to a TYPE object that describes how to interpret this object.

### Graceful Degradation

The network remains usable with chunks missing:
- Signature doubles as integrity check (CRC-like)
- No strict hash-chains that break when one object is missing
- Indexes and discovery use time-based chunking (resilient to gaps)
- Related objects are nice-to-have, not hard dependencies

### Subgraphs and Independence

Not everything needs to link from the root. You are free to start your own subgraphs.

### Linking to Root

Structural objects (identities, communities, specs, types) should include a `root` relation pointing to the ROOT object. Content objects (posts, comments) don't need this - they're reachable through structural objects.
